findings.claim: The evaluation design assumes that report-level recall (fraction of reports with at least one correct follow-up recommendation span) is sufficient to measure clinical screening utility and that secondary metrics (per-entity F1, span-level precision/recall) adequately characterize extraction fidelity.
findings.failure_mechanism: Report-level recall may inflate performance by rewarding models that detect any follow-up span, even if it is incorrect or irrelevant (e.g., detecting a span in a negated or conditional context). This metric does not penalize false positives at the report level, only at the span level via secondary metrics. Clinically, a false positive (e.g., flagging a report as containing a follow-up recommendation when it does not) could trigger unnecessary downstream actions, which report-level recall does not capture. The secondary metrics are not weighted to reflect clinical utility, and span-level precision/recall may not align with the primary objective of not missing actionable reports.
findings.evidence_test: Compare report-level recall to a clinically weighted metric that penalizes false positives at the report level (e.g., a metric that counts a report as a false positive if any detected span is incorrect or irrelevant). Alternatively, perform a failure analysis on false positives to quantify the fraction that are clinically harmful (e.g., negated or conditional language). If report-level recall is high but the clinically weighted metric is low, the primary metric is misleading.

findings.claim: The evaluation design assumes that the held-out cross-site test set (one hospital system) is sufficient to test cross-site generalization.
findings.failure_mechanism: The cross-site test set may not capture the full range of distribution shifts present in production (e.g., different scanners, radiologist styles, report templates, or clinical workflows). A single hospital system may not represent the diversity of deployment environments. The design does not include a second held-out system or a stress test for extreme shifts (e.g., non-English reports, pediatric vs. adult reports, or reports from systems with vastly different EHR integration). This limits the generalizability of the cross-site results.
findings.evidence_test: Evaluate the model on additional held-out systems or synthetic distribution shifts (e.g., adversarial templates, noisy OCR, or reports from systems with different clinical guidelines). If performance degrades significantly on these, the cross-site test set is insufficient.

findings.claim: The evaluation design assumes that the CRF baseline (TF-IDF + hand-engineered features) is a fair and adequate comparison for the Transformer model.
findings.failure_mechanism: The CRF baseline may be underpowered or misconfigured. TF-IDF features are sensitive to vocabulary mismatch and may not capture the semantic nuances of follow-up recommendations (e.g., negations, conditionals, or domain-specific phrasing). Hand-engineered features (e.g., keyword lists) may be brittle or incomplete, especially for rare but clinically important phrases. The CRF's linear-chain assumption may also limit its ability to model long-range dependencies in radiology reports. Without a stronger baseline (e.g., a modern sequence model like BiLSTM-CRF or a pretrained language model fine-tuned for token classification), the comparison does not establish whether the Transformer's advantage is due to architecture or simply better feature engineering.
findings.evidence_test: Compare against a stronger baseline (e.g., BiLSTM-CRF with contextual embeddings or a smaller Transformer like DistilBERT). If the Transformer's advantage persists, the claim of superiority is stronger. Alternatively, perform an ablation where the Transformer is restricted to TF-IDF + hand-engineered features; if performance drops to CRF levels, the baseline is adequate.

findings.claim: The evaluation design assumes that the annotation guidelines and inter-rater agreement (Cohen's kappa ≥0.75) are sufficient to ensure label quality for training and evaluation.
findings.failure_mechanism: Radiology reports contain complex language (negations, conditionals, abbreviations, and domain-specific jargon), and even high inter-rater agreement may not capture systematic biases in annotation. For example, annotators may agree on the presence of a follow-up recommendation but disagree on its span boundaries or entity type. This could lead to label noise that disproportionately affects the Transformer (which relies on precise token boundaries) compared to the CRF (which may be more robust to boundary errors). The design does not include a label quality audit for entity-level agreement or span boundary consistency.
findings.evidence_test: Compute entity-level and span-level inter-rater agreement (e.g., using F1 for span boundaries) on the gold-standard sample. If agreement is low, perform adjudication and relabeling. Alternatively, measure the impact of label noise by training the Transformer on noisy labels and evaluating on clean labels (or vice versa).

findings.claim: The evaluation design assumes that the temporal split (oldest 60% train, next 20% validation, most recent 20% test) is sufficient to test generalization to future reporting trends.
findings.failure_mechanism: Radiology reporting practices and templates evolve over time due to changes in clinical guidelines, EHR systems, or hospital policies. The temporal split may not capture sudden shifts (e.g., a new template introduced mid-year) or gradual drifts (e.g., increasing use of abbreviations). The design does not include a drift detection mechanism (e.g., monitoring vocabulary shifts or template similarity over time) to ensure the test set is representative of future data.
findings.evidence_test: Monitor vocabulary drift (e.g., using KL divergence on unigram distributions) or template similarity (e.g., 4-gram overlap) between train/validation and test sets over time. If drift is detected, stratify the test set by time and evaluate performance separately for each stratum. If performance degrades in later strata, the temporal split is insufficient.

findings.claim: The evaluation design assumes that the CRF's hand-engineered features (e.g., follow-up keywords, token length, position) are sufficient to capture the linguistic patterns of follow-up recommendations.
findings.failure_mechanism: Hand-engineered features may miss nuanced linguistic patterns (e.g., negations like 'no follow-up recommended' or conditionals like 'if symptoms persist'). The CRF's performance may be artificially low due to feature limitations rather than the model's capacity. The design does not include an analysis of which features contribute most to performance or whether the feature set is comprehensive.
findings.evidence_test: Perform feature ablation studies (e.g., remove keywords, position features, or case information) and measure the impact on CRF performance. Alternatively, train the CRF on contextual embeddings (e.g., averaged word2vec) to isolate the impact of hand-engineered features.

critic_r1_output.summary: The PoC has several fatal flaws that invalidate its central claims: (1) the primary metric (report-level recall) does not align with clinical utility and may inflate performance, (2) the cross-site test set is insufficient to test generalization, and (3) the CRF baseline may be inadequate. Material flaws include unverified label quality and untested distribution shifts. These issues must be addressed before the hypothesis can be evaluated.

defender_r1_output.pass_1_analysis:
F1 claims report-level recall is insufficient because it doesn't penalize false positives (FPs) at the report level. However, the design explicitly defines the clinical goal as 'not missing reports' for registry creation (screening), where recall is the primary driver. Precision is addressed via secondary metrics (span-level precision/recall). The critic's suggestion to penalize FPs at the report level essentially describes a different metric (like report-level F1 or Precision), but for a screening tool, high recall is the intentional priority. This is a design choice based on the clinical use case.

F2 argues that one held-out hospital system is insufficient for cross-site generalization. The design uses 3-4 systems total. Holding out one entire system (approx 5k-8k reports) is a standard PoC approach to test out-of-distribution (OOD) performance. The critic suggests testing non-English or pediatric reports, which are explicitly outside the stated scope (Section 7). Testing one distinct hospital system is a valid proxy for site-generalization in a PoC.

F3 argues the CRF baseline is too weak. The design includes a 'Representation confound' ablation (Section 6) where the Transformer is run using CRF-compatible features. This directly addresses whether the gain is from the architecture or the features. Furthermore, the goal is to compare a modern Transformer against a traditional baseline (TF-IDF+CRF) to justify the complexity of the former; a BiLSTM-CRF is a middle-ground, but the current baseline is a standard 'traditional' benchmark.

F4 concerns label quality and span boundaries. The design establishes inter-rater agreement (Kappa >= 0.75) and uses a senior clinician for adjudication. The primary metric (report-level recall) was specifically chosen to 'reduce sensitivity to small span-boundary differences' (Section 5). This design choice directly mitigates the risk of boundary noise affecting the primary conclusion.

F5 claims the temporal split doesn't detect drift. The design explicitly includes 'Template leakage' controls (character 4-gram similarity) and 'out-of-vocabulary rates' (Section 3 and 6) to detect distribution shift. These are the standard mechanisms for monitoring the drift the critic is concerned about.

F6 argues the CRF features are insufficient. This is a duplicate of the concern in F3. The 'Representation confound' ablation mentioned in Section 6 resolves this by isolating the benefit of the Transformer's representations versus the CRF's features.

rebuttals.justification: The primary metric (report-level recall) is a deliberate design choice aligned with the stated clinical goal: 'not missing reports that contain actionable follow-up language for downstream registry creation.' In a screening context, missing a case (False Negative) is significantly more costly than a False Positive. Precision is tracked via secondary span-level metrics to ensure the model isn't simply predicting 'True' for every report.

The critic suggests testing non-English or pediatric reports, which are explicitly excluded in the Scope (Section 7). Testing on one entirely held-out hospital system (5k-8k reports) is a sufficient and rigorous test of cross-site generalization for a PoC hypothesis investigation.

The design includes a specific 'Representation confound' ablation (Section 6) that runs the Transformer using CRF-compatible token-level features. This eliminates the mechanism of 'better feature engineering' as the sole cause of improvement, isolating the architectural benefit.

The choice of report-level recall as the primary metric was specifically made to 'reduce sensitivity to small span-boundary differences' (Section 5), which directly resolves the concern that boundary noise would invalidate the primary conclusion.
The design already includes drift detection mechanisms: monitoring out-of-vocabulary rates (Section 3) and computing character 4-gram similarity to flag template leakage/shift (Section 6).
As with F3, the 'Representation confound' ablation (Section 6) isolates whether the performance gap is due to the CRF's feature limitations or the Transformer's capacity.

defender_r1_output.verdict_rationale: Most findings were rebutted via existing design controls. However, F1 remains at adjusted severity 4 because while the design choice is justified for screening, the magnitude of the False Positive rate's impact on clinical utility is an empirical question that cannot be resolved by argument alone.

challenges.reasoning: The defender argues that report-level recall aligns with clinical screening goals and that precision is tracked via secondary metrics. While this addresses the high-level concern, it does not fully resolve the failure mechanism: report-level recall still does not penalize false positives at the report level, and secondary metrics (e.g., span-level precision) may not reflect clinical harm (e.g., negated or conditional language). The rebuttal does not address the specific mechanism where a high recall could mask clinically harmful false positives by relying solely on secondary metrics. The design choice is reasonable but does not eliminate the metric's limitations.

The defender correctly notes that non-English or pediatric reports are explicitly out of scope (Section 7), and testing on one held-out hospital system is sufficient for a PoC investigation. The rebuttal directly addresses the failure mechanism by clarifying scope and justifying the sufficiency of the cross-site test set.

The defender cites the 'Representation confound' ablation (Section 6), which isolates the Transformer's architectural benefit by running it with CRF-compatible features. This directly addresses the failure mechanism by demonstrating that the performance gap is not solely due to better feature engineering. The rebuttal is mechanistically sound.

The defender argues that report-level recall reduces sensitivity to span-boundary differences, which partially addresses the concern. However, it does not fully resolve the issue of systematic biases in annotation (e.g., entity-level agreement or span boundary consistency). The rebuttal acknowledges the metric choice but does not address the label quality audit or entity-level agreement, leaving residual concerns about label noise disproportionately affecting the Transformer.

The defender cites the inclusion of drift detection mechanisms (out-of-vocabulary rates and 4-gram similarity in Section 6), which directly addresses the failure mechanism by monitoring distribution shift. The rebuttal is mechanistically sound and resolves the concern.

The defender cites the 'Representation confound' ablation (Section 6), which isolates the impact of hand-engineered features by training the Transformer with CRF-compatible features. This directly addresses the failure mechanism by demonstrating whether the CRF's feature limitations are the primary cause of its performance gap. The rebuttal is mechanistically sound.

defender_r2_output.pass_2_analysis: I am maintaining my positions on F2, F3, F5, and F6 as they were accepted by the critic. For F1 and F4, the critic issued PARTIAL verdicts. For F1, the critic argues that report-level recall does not penalize clinically harmful false positives (e.g., negated spans). Since the design only mitigates this via secondary metrics rather than eliminating the mechanism, I am switching to DEFER. For F4, the critic argues that while the metric reduces boundary sensitivity, it doesn't address systematic annotation bias or entity-level agreement. Since the design lacks a specific label quality audit for entity-level consistency, I must CONCEDE this as an absent control.

rebuttals.justification: The critic is correct that report-level recall does not penalize false positives at the report level, and secondary span-level metrics may not capture the clinical impact of 'harmful' false positives (e.g., flagging a negated recommendation as actionable). The design's use of secondary metrics mitigates but does not eliminate this risk. To settle this, we will perform a targeted failure analysis on all report-level false positives: a clinician will review these cases to categorize the error (e.g., 'negation missed', 'conditional misinterpreted', or 'irrelevant span'). If >20% of report-level false positives are due to missed negations/conditionals, the primary metric is validated as misleading, and we will adopt a 'clinically-weighted report-level F1' that penalizes these specific errors.

The design's scope is explicitly limited to English-language radiology reports (Section 7). Testing on one entirely held-out hospital system (5k-8k reports) is a rigorous and sufficient test of cross-site generalization for the purposes of this hypothesis investigation.

The 'Representation confound' ablation (Section 6) explicitly runs the Transformer using CRF-compatible token-level features. This isolates the architectural benefit from the feature engineering benefit, directly addressing the failure mechanism.

The critic is correct. While the choice of report-level recall reduces sensitivity to boundary noise, the methodology lacks a specific label quality audit for entity-level agreement or systematic bias across the full training set. There is no design control to ensure that annotation noise does not disproportionately affect the Transformer's token-level learning.
The design includes explicit drift detection via out-of-vocabulary rate monitoring (Section 3) and character 4-gram similarity checks (Section 6) to flag template evolution or leakage.

The 'Representation confound' ablation (Section 6) isolates whether the performance gap is due to the CRF's feature limitations or the Transformer's capacity by restricting the Transformer to the same feature set.

defender_r2_output.verdict_rationale: While several findings were successfully rebutted, F1 remains a significant uncertainty that requires a targeted failure analysis (DEFER), and F4 was conceded. The presence of a DEFER on a FATAL finding makes this an empirical test agreement.
